does: This paper proposes Gradient Token Masking (GTM), a defense mechanism against visual prompt injection attacks on multimodal large language models. GTM localizes adversarial tokens by computing gradients with respect to a Hidden-State Gradient Norm score—a metric that measures how much each token influences generation under attack. The method then neutralizes identified critical tokens through masking. The authors demonstrate theoretically that their attribution metric's token ranking is consistent with the full adversarial loss gradient, ensuring accurate localization. GTM operates efficiently with a single forward-backward pass and masks only a small subset of tokens to disrupt adversarial effects while preserving benign model behavior.
connects: This work addresses the emerging threat of adversarial attacks on vision-language models, extending prior research on adversarial robustness and prompt injection vulnerabilities in multimodal systems. It builds on gradient-based attribution and token-level interpretability methods used in analyzing transformer models, while responding to limitations in existing defenses that struggle to balance computational cost with security guarantees. The paper connects to broader work on understanding how adversarial perturbations exploit model vulnerabilities and leverages insights from adversarial loss analysis to pinpoint critical image regions. It also relates to token pruning and selective attention literature, applying these concepts defensively rather than for efficiency gains.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more principled, interpretable defenses against adversarial attacks on vision-language systems. By establishing a connection between gradient-based attribution and adversarial robustness, it provides a foundation for designing defense mechanisms with theoretical guarantees. The approach enables efficient deployment since it requires minimal computational overhead compared to other defense strategies. Future work could extend GTM to handle adaptive attacks designed specifically against gradient-based defenses, investigate combinations with other defense layers, or develop stronger attribution methods that resist gradient-masking attacks. The framework also suggests broader applications in securing other multimodal architectures and understanding which model components are most vulnerable to adversarial manipulation.
